title: Meows 🐱 The Language Cats Invented For You
description: Adult cats almost never meow at each other. Meows are a language developed exclusively to communicate with humans. Science has identified over 20 different types of meows with distinct meanings. Discover how your cat talks to you and what they're trying to say based on real studies.

segments.voice: Adult cats almost never meow at each other. They use body language, scents, and growls. The meow is a language cats developed exclusively to communicate with humans. Researchers at Lund University in Sweden identified over twenty-one distinct types of meows, each with a different meaning. Your cat literally learned to talk to you.
segments.visual: cat meowing at owner looking up

segments.voice: Welcome to CatBrothers. Today we'll decode the most sophisticated language an animal has developed to communicate with another species. Cat meows aren't random noise. They're a communication system that evolved over ten thousand years of coexistence with humans. Science is beginning to decode it, and the results are extraordinary.
segments.visual: cat opening mouth meowing close up

segments.voice: Let's start with the fundamental fact. Kittens meow at their mother to ask for attention, food, or help. As they grow, they stop doing this. Feral adult cats practically never meow. But domestic cats maintain and refine their meows throughout their lives. Dr. Sharon Crowell-Davis from the University of Georgia calls this vocal neoteny: retaining an infantile behavior into adulthood.
segments.visual: kitten meowing at mother cat

segments.voice: Researcher Susanne Schötz from Lund University led the Melody project between 2016 and 2021, the most comprehensive study on feline vocalization. She recorded and acoustically analyzed thousands of meows from seventy cats. She discovered that cats have a repertoire of at least twenty-one distinct vocal patterns, each with measurable and reproducible acoustic characteristics.
segments.visual: sound wave audio recording equipment

segments.voice: Meows are divided into three main categories according to Schötz. First, closed-mouth sounds: purring and trilling. Second, open-mouth sounds that close: the classic meow. Third, tense open-mouth sounds: hissing, yowling, and growling. Each category conveys a different emotional range, from affection to aggression.
segments.visual: cat making different vocal expressions

segments.voice: The trill or chirp is perhaps the most positive vocalization. It's an ascending sound the cat makes with its mouth closed. Schötz documented that cats use trills as greetings, to express joy when seeing their owner, and as invitations to follow them. Mothers trill to call their kittens. It's the feline equivalent of a loving hello.
segments.visual: cat chirping trilling greeting owner door

segments.voice: The food-request meow is acoustically different from the attention-request meow. A 2009 study by Karen McComb from the University of Sussex, published in Current Biology, showed that the food meow contains an embedded high-pitched frequency between 300 and 600 Hz, which activates the same brain areas as a human baby's cry.
segments.visual: cat meowing near food bowl kitchen

segments.voice: McComb called this the solicitation purr. It's pure acoustic manipulation. Cats learned that including this urgent frequency makes humans respond faster. When this frequency was digitally removed from the audio, humans perceived the meow as less urgent. Cats have hacked our parental instincts.
segments.visual: person rushing to feed meowing cat

segments.voice: Cats develop individual dialects with their owners. A Cornell University study by Dr. Nicholas Nicastro analyzed one hundred meows from twelve cats and found that owners could correctly identify the context of their own cat's meow 73% of the time. But with unfamiliar cats' meows, the accuracy dropped to 40%.
segments.visual: owner and cat communicating understanding

segments.voice: This means each cat develops a customized vocabulary with their human. Your cat has a specific meow for you that they don't use with others. Dr. Sharon Crowell-Davis explains that cats adjust the frequency, duration, and intensity of their meows based on which vocalizations get the best response from their particular owner.
segments.visual: cat looking at specific person meowing

segments.voice: Siamese and Oriental cats are notoriously more vocal. A comparative study from the University of Helsinki analyzed over 4,400 cats from 26 breeds and confirmed that Siamese cats vocalize three times more frequently than Persians or British Shorthairs. Genetics significantly influence meowing tendencies.
segments.visual: siamese cat meowing loudly vocal

segments.voice: Nighttime yowling in older cats may indicate feline cognitive dysfunction, the equivalent of human Alzheimer's. A University of Edinburgh study published in the Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery found that 36% of cats over eleven show signs of cognitive decline, and excessive nighttime vocalization is one of the first symptoms.
segments.visual: senior cat meowing at night confused

segments.voice: The chattering or chirping cats make when they see a bird through the window is a unique vocalization. Their teeth chatter rapidly while making a staccato sound. A 2010 study by the Federal University of Juiz de Fora in Brazil proposed this might be an example of acoustic mimicry: cats imitating their prey's sounds to lure them.
segments.visual: cat chattering at bird through window

segments.voice: This mimicry hypothesis emerged when researchers observed a wild margay in the Amazon mimicking the sounds of a tamarin monkey to lure it. If wild felines mimic prey, domestic cats might retain this instinct when chattering at birds. The research is in early stages, but the evidence is curious.
segments.visual: wild cat hunting in jungle mimicry

segments.voice: Hissing is a defensive vocalization that mimics a snake. The cat opens its mouth, flattens its ears, and expels air sharply. Dr. John Bradshaw from the University of Bristol suggests hissing evolved as a form of Batesian mimicry: pretending to be a dangerous animal to avoid confrontation. Predators that fear snakes would retreat from a feline hiss.
segments.visual: cat hissing showing teeth defensive

segments.voice: Meow frequency varies dramatically. A University of Vienna study measured meows between 300-600 Hz in normal situations, but up to 1,000 Hz in extreme stress. Higher-pitched meows indicate greater urgency. Cats instinctively know high-pitched sounds grab more human attention.
segments.visual: stressed cat meowing loudly high pitch

segments.voice: Deaf cats also meow, but differently. A University of North Carolina study found that congenitally deaf cats produce meows with irregular frequencies and patterns because they can't hear themselves to adjust their voice. This confirms that hearing cats actively modulate their vocalizations based on auditory feedback.
segments.visual: white deaf cat meowing silently

segments.voice: Artificial intelligence is decoding meows. The MeowTalk project, developed by a former Amazon Alexa employee, uses machine learning to classify meows into categories like hunger, play, stress, and greeting. Though reported accuracy is limited, University of Milan researchers are training more sophisticated models with databases of thousands of labeled vocalizations.
segments.visual: smartphone app translating cat meow

segments.voice: Cats also communicate with ultrasounds. A University of Bern study detected ultrasonic components in kitten meows that are beyond human hearing range. These components might serve for mother-offspring communication without alerting predators. Kittens might be saying things we'll never hear.
segments.visual: mother cat listening to kittens closely

segments.voice: Human response to meows has been studied by neurolinguistics. A University of Sussex study using functional MRI showed that meows activate language-processing brain areas in experienced owners. Literally, our brains process cat meows as if they were a form of language.
segments.visual: person listening to cat meow attentively

segments.voice: Cats don't meow into a void. A University of Portsmouth study found cats meow significantly more when they detect humans are looking at them. If the human is turned away or absent, meowing frequency drops drastically. Cats calibrate their vocal communication to their audience's perceived attention.
segments.visual: cat making eye contact then meowing

segments.voice: Purring with an embedded meow is a particularly effective combination. Dr. McComb documented that cats combine both vocalizations to create an irresistible sound: soothing from the purr but urgent from the meow's high-pitched frequency. It's the most sophisticated acoustic manipulation tool in the feline repertoire.
segments.visual: cat purring and meowing mixed sounds

segments.voice: Cats also vocalize during play. Short, high-pitched sounds during chasing, trills during capture, and soft meows during pauses. An ethological study from the University of Zurich classified six distinct play vocalizations, each associated with a different phase of the simulated hunting sequence.
segments.visual: cat playing vocalizing chasing toy

segments.voice: After ten thousand years of coexistence, cats have created a language exclusively for us. They don't use it with each other. Each cat develops a customized vocabulary with their owner. And science is proving our brains process it as a real language. You're not crazy when you say you understand your cat. Science backs you up.
segments.visual: cat and owner face to face bonding

segments.voice: This was the last episode of the season, and it's been an honor sharing feline science with you. If you want us to keep telling striking cat stories, subscribe to CatBrothers now and hit the bell. Share this so more people discover how striking cats are. See you next season.
segments.visual: happy cat waving paw at camera
